title: The Woman Taken in Adultery
subtitle: Jesus stoops, writes in the dust, and condemns no one

stillness: He stooped down, and bent low to the ground.
He turned his face away, that the guilty might not blush to leave.
Bend down with him now, into silence.
gospel: Early the next day Jesus came again into the temple,
and all the people came to him; and sitting down, he taught them.
The scribes and the Pharisees brought to him a woman taken in adultery,
and they set her in the midst,
tempting him, that they might accuse him.
But Jesus, stooping down, wrote with his finger on the ground.
When they continued asking him, he lifted himself up and said:
“He that is without sin among you, let him first cast a stone at her.”
And again stooping down, he wrote on the ground.
And they, hearing it, went out one by one.
behold: Behold the scribes, who held the knowledge of the Scriptures,
and the Pharisees, who seemed more religious than the rest,
envying him and lying in wait,
who knew him to be gentle, a preacher of mercy.
They bring to him a woman taken in adultery,
to be stoned according to the Law,
and they set her in the midst, that by every mouth she be condemned to death.
They await Christ’s sentence:
if he should judge her to be stoned, they would mock him as cruel, a man without the mercy he preached;
if he should decree her set free, they would judge him a breaker of the Law.
But Jesus prudently escaped both snares.
He did not judge at once;
but first, bending himself down, low and humbly from the rigor of justice,
he wrote with his finger in the earth.
And again stooping down, he wrote the same once more in the ground,
as if he said: If she is a sinner, you also are sinners.
And when he had struck them with the zeal of justice,
he did not deign to look upon them, but turned his gaze away,
the kind Lord, even for his rivals and adversaries,
sparing their shame, that they might depart freely.
For if he had looked them in the face, they would have blushed the more to leave.
consider: This he wrote, as Augustine says: that such men are to be written in the earth, not in heaven.
By the earth is shown the human heart, which renders the fruit of good deeds or of evil.
He teaches us, when we hear the evils of our neighbor, not rashly and at once to judge them,
but first, humbly returning to the conscience of the heart, to search them out with the finger of discernment;
and to examine our very selves within, whether we are without sin.
“He that is without sin” — he says without sin, not without crime:
for a man may be without crime, but scarcely without sin.
As if he said: Let each one ascend the tribunal of his own mind, and he will find himself a sinner.
Either let this woman go, or together with her receive the penalty of the Law.
Let the sinful woman be punished, but not by sinners;
let the Law you allege be fulfilled, but not by breakers of the Law.
First be just; afterward punish the guilty.
If anyone is in mortal sin, he ought to judge no one;
for he who judges another for that to which he himself is bound, condemns himself in this.
Hear Seneca on this: Show yourself first to be good, and so seek another like yourself.
See whether you are still evil, and spare those who are like you.
So, before we judge anyone, let us look upon our own conscience,
and weigh ourselves by a just examination.
prayer: who didst mercifully free the woman taken in adultery from her accusers,
and, leaving her uncondemned, didst send her away in peace:
before thee stands my soul, an adulteress,
who has departed from thee, the true Spouse, as often as she consented to the suggestions of the adulterous enemy.
My conscience accuses me; my works and wicked deeds accuse me.
Enter not, O Lord, into judgment with her,
free the sinner and the guilty one from her accusers;
dismiss in peace her conscience, absolved from thy dread judgment,
for it is proper to thee always to have mercy and to spare,
and there is no number to thy mercy. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Venit enim non perdere quod invenerit, sed quærere quod perierat.
latinJewel.english: For he came not to destroy what he had found, but to seek what had perished.
